perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information' shall mean all information or data disclosed (whether in writing, orally or b y any other means) to one party by the other party or by a third party on behalf of the other party and shall include but not be limited to (A) any information ascertainable by the inspection or analysis of samples, (B) the information described in the Sch edule 1 hereto as having been disclosed prior to the date hereof and (C) any information relating to that party's business, operations, prices, processes, plans, intentions, product information, know - how, design rights, trade secrets, software, market oppo rtunities, customers and business affairs, but shall exclude any part of such disclosed information or data which: (i) is or becomes in the public domain in any way without breach of this Agreement by the receiving party; or - 2 - (ii) the receiving party can show (A) was in its possession or known to it by being in its use or being recorded in its files or computers or other recording media prior to receipt from the disclosing party and was not previously acquired by the receiving party from the disclosing party under an obligation of confidence; or (B) to have been developed by or for the receiving party at any time independently of the information disclosed to it by the disclosing party; or (iii) is hereafter disclosed or made available to the receiving party from a source other than the disclosing party without breach by the receiving party or such source of any obligation of confidentiality or non -use towards the disclosing party; or (iv) is hereafter made generally available by the disclosing party or a third party or is discl osed by the disclosing party to a third party without restriction on disclosure or use, including, without limitation, by way of the publication of a patent specification; or (v) is disclosed by the receiving party with the prior written appr oval of the disclo sing party; provided however that the foregoing exceptions shall not apply to information relating to any combination of features or any combination of items of information merely because information relating to one or more of the relevant individual featu res or one or more of the relevant items (but not the combination itself) falls within any one or more of such exceptions.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information' shall mean all information or data disclosed (whether in writing, orally or b y any other means) to one party by the other party or by a third party on behalf of the other party and shall include but not be limited to (A) any information ascertainable by the inspection or analysis of samples, (B) the information described in the Sch edule 1 hereto as having been disclosed prior to the date hereof and (C) any information relating to that party's business, operations, prices, processes, plans, intentions, product information, know - how, design rights, trade secrets, software, market oppo rtunities, customers and business affairs, but shall exclude any part of such disclosed information or data which: (i) is or becomes in the public domain in any way without breach of this Agreement by the receiving party; or - 2 - (ii) the receiving party can show it was publicly available information prior to receipt; or (iii) is hereafter disclosed or made available to the receiving party from a source other than the disclosing party without breach by the receiving party or such source of any obligation of confidentiality or non -use towards the disclosing party; or (iv) is hereafter made generally available by the disclosing party or a third party or is discl osed by the disclosing party to a third party without restriction on disclosure or use, including, without limitation, by way of the publication of a patent specification; or (v) is disclosed by the receiving party with the prior written appr oval of the disclo sing party; provided however that the foregoing exceptions shall not apply to information relating to any combination of features or any combination of items of information merely because information relating to one or more of the relevant individual featu res or one or more of the relevant items (but not the combination itself) falls within any one or more of such exceptions.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Confidential Information' is altered to exclude information that the receiving party can show was 'publicly available information prior to receipt'. This conflicts with the existing, more specific exclusion criteria related to prior possession and independent development, and could allow the receiving party to claim broader exceptions to confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 3(1)(a) defines a trade secret as information that is 'not generally known nor readily accessible to persons within the circles that normally deal with the kind of information in question'. The modified clause potentially allows the receiving party to exclude information as 'publicly available' even if it is not readily accessible, thus contradicting the Directive's definition of a trade secret.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text broadens the exceptions to 'Confidential Information' by including 'publicly available information prior to receipt.' This contradicts the intent, as expressed in the Law Explanation. The Law Explanation, referring to Article 3(1)(a), defines a trade secret as information that is 'not generally known nor readily accessible'. By allowing the exclusion of merely 'publicly available information,' the modified clause undermines the protection of trade secrets that might be publicly available but not readily accessible, deviating from the legally recognized definitions. The original text's exclusion criteria were more specific, related to prior possession and independent development, while the changed text opens a broader avenue for excluding information, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to keep the existence and nature of this Agreemen t confidential and not to use the same or the name of the other party (or of any other company in the Group of Companies of which the other party forms part) in any publicity, advertisement or other disclosure with regard to this Agreement without the prio r written consent of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to keep the existence and nature of this Agreemen t confidential, except if required by standard business practices, and not to use the same or the name of the other party (or of any other company in the Group of Companies of which the other party forms part) in any publicity, advertisement or other disclosure with regard to this Agreement without the prio r written consent of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: The added phrase 'except if required by standard business practices' introduces ambiguity and undermines the confidentiality clause. 'Standard business practices' is vague and could be interpreted in ways that allow for disclosures prohibited by trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4(2) addresses the unlawful acquisition, use and disclosure of trade secrets, including situations where a party 'knew or ought to have known that the trade secret had been obtained directly or indirectly from another person who was unlawfully using or disclosing the trade secret'. The phrase 'standard business practices' offers a loophole for potential unlawful disclosures, which conflicts with this article.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces an exception ('except if required by standard business practices') to the confidentiality agreement, which contradicts the intent of protecting trade secrets as outlined in Directive (EU) 2016/943. The original text ensures complete confidentiality, while the changed text allows for disclosures under the vague umbrella of 'standard business practices'. This ambiguity weakens the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information, potentially leading to unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure as mentioned in the law snippet. The scraped snippet specifically mentions 'the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets) against their unlawful acquisition, use and disclosure', highlighting the legal concern that the modified text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Disclosure in breach of this Agreement will result in irreparable injury to the disclosing party for which monetary damages alone will not be an adequate remedy. Therefore, the parties agree that in the event of a breach or threatened breach of the terms of this Agreement, the disclosing party will be entitled to specific performance, injunctive relief or other equitable relief prohibiting any breach of this Agreement without giving an undertaking as to damages. Any such equitable remedy shall b e in addition to and not in lieu of, other appropriate relief at law to which the disclosing party may be entitled.
perturbation.changed_text: Disclosure in breach of this Agreement will result in possible injury to the disclosing party for which monetary damages alone may not be an adequate remedy. Therefore, the parties agree that in the event of a breach or threatened breach of the terms of this Agreement, the disclosing party will be entitled to seek specific performance, injunctive relief or other equitable relief prohibiting any breach of this Agreement with a requirement of an undertaking as to damages. Any such equitable remedy shall b e in addition to and not in lieu of, other appropriate relief at law to which the disclosing party may be entitled.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'irreparable injury' to 'possible injury' and adding 'seek' and 'with a requirement of an undertaking as to damages', the contract weakens the provision for equitable relief. This undermines the protection afforded to confidential information, as the disclosing party must now demonstrate actual damages and potentially provide an undertaking, which may be difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 10 specifies the measures, procedures and remedies to ensure that civil redress is available against the unlawful acquisition, use and disclosure of trade secrets. The modification weakens the equitable relief clause by changing “irreparable injury” to “possible injury,” thereby potentially limiting the availability of effective remedies such as injunctive relief without an undertaking of damages, which contradicts the directive's aim to provide effective remedies.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract language weakens the protection against unlawful disclosure of trade secrets, potentially contradicting the directive's goal of ensuring civil redress, as stated in the Law Explanation. By changing 'irreparable injury' to 'possible injury' and adding 'with a requirement of an undertaking as to damages', the contract introduces conditions that could limit the availability of effective remedies, as the disclosing party now has a higher burden of proof and may need to provide an undertaking. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Directive (EU) 2016/943, which concerns the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets) against their unlawful acquisition, use and disclosure. The change weakens the equitable relief clause, potentially making it more difficult for the disclosing party to obtain injunctive relief, undermining the directive's aim to provide effective remedies against the unlawful disclosure of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The interpretation construction and effect of this Agreement shall be governed and construed in all respects in accordance with English Law and the parties submit to the exclusive jurisdictio n of the courts of England and Wales.
perturbation.changed_text: The interpretation construction and effect of this Agreement shall be governed and construed in all respects in accordance with general principles of law and the parties submit to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of any court.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'English Law and the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales' to 'general principles of law and the non-exclusive jurisdiction of any court' creates significant uncertainty. 'General principles of law' is vague and could lead to conflicting interpretations. 'Non-exclusive jurisdiction' allows disputes to be heard in multiple jurisdictions, potentially undermining the intended application of English law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 23 allows parties to agree on a specific jurisdiction in case of disputes. By changing the governing law to 'general principles of law' and removing the exclusive jurisdiction clause, the contract now contradicts the specific jurisdiction agreement.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'English Law and the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales,' which is a clear agreement on governing law and jurisdiction. The changed text replaces this with 'general principles of law and the non-exclusive jurisdiction of any court.' According to the perturbation explanation, 'General principles of law' is vague and could lead to conflicting interpretations, and 'Non-exclusive jurisdiction' allows disputes to be heard in multiple jurisdictions, potentially undermining the intended application of English law. The Law Explanation suggests that this contradicts the ability of parties to agree on a specific jurisdiction. While the scraped snippets provide limited direct support, the core issue is that the changed text undermines the original agreement on a specific, enforceable jurisdiction, thus creating a legal contradiction under the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' as the changed terminology does not match its statutory meaning, making the contract unenforceable. The change removes a specific legal framework and replaces it with a vague and open-ended one.

perturbation.original_text: Each party warrants its right to disclose its Confidential Information to the other party and to authorise the other party to use the same for the Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party believes it has the right to disclose its Confidential Information to the other party and to authorize the other party to use the same for the Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'warrants' with 'believes it has' weakens the assurance given by each party. A warranty is a legally binding promise, while a belief is subjective and doesn't carry the same weight. This could create a situation where a party discloses information they don't have the right to disclose, leading to legal issues.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 2(1) of the Misrepresentation Act 1967 deals with damages for misrepresentation. If a party 'warrants' their right to disclose but does not actually have it, they can be held liable under the Act. By changing this to 'believes it has', the disclosing party avoids a warranty and thus significantly reduces their potential liability for misrepresentation, which conflicts with the intent of protecting parties from negligent misstatements about their rights.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Misrepresentation Act 1967 | LawTeacher.net Loading Disclaimer: This work was produced by one of our expert legal writers, as a learning aid to help law students with their studies. View full disclaimer Any opinions, findings, conclusions, or recommendations expressed in this material are those of the authors and do not reflect the views of LawTeacher.net. Any information contained in this act summary does not constitute legal advice and should be treated as educational content only. Accept and close Share this: Facebook Twitter Reddit LinkedIn WhatsApp Brief Background The Misrepresentation Act 1967 is an Act which governs English contract law in the United Kingdom in situations where misrepresentation is involved, prior to parties entering into a contractual agreement. A misrepresentation can be considered to be a false statement of fact which prompts an individual to enter into a contract on the basis of that false statement. Misrepresentation can be established with three different types of statement. These are fraudulent, negligent and innocent misrepresentations and are often considered tiered in terms of severity by the courts. Why was it introduced? Misrepresentation is an important factor in both contract law and tort law in the English legal system. The Misrepresentation Act 1967 ensures that parties have an action to pursue in both contract law as well as the law of tort if the misrepresentation causes loss to one of the parties. Moreover, the Misrepresentation Act 1967 allows for the parties to be placed back into the same position that they would have been in before the contract was constructed as if a misrepresentation is found a contract can be effectively voided. This rule applies whether the misrepresentation was fraudulent, negligent or an innocent mistake. What was the aim of the Act? The introduction of the Misrepresentation Act 1967 was meant to provide a greater amount of security to parties that enter into a contractual agreement, to ensure that they are not tied to an
perturbation.justification: The original text provides a warranty, which is a legally binding assurance. The changed text weakens this assurance by replacing 'warrants' with 'believes it has', thus reducing potential liability for misrepresentation. According to the Law Explanation, Section 2(1) of the Misrepresentation Act 1967 addresses damages for misrepresentation and provides a basis for liability when a party warrants something they don't have the right to. By weakening the language to 'believes it has', the party avoids such warranty and potential liability, conflicting with the intent of protecting parties from negligent misstatements. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, "The Misrepresentation Act 1967 ensures that parties have an action to pursue in both contract law as well as the law of tort if the misrepresentation causes loss to one of the parties." The change weakens the assurance and therefore undermines the Act's intention to ensure security to parties entering contractual agreements.

perturbation.original_text: All notices under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be sent by first -class registered or recorded delivery post to the party being served at its address specified above or at such other address of which such party shall have given notice as aforesaid, and marked for the attention of that party's signatory of this Agreement. The date of service shall be deemed to be the day following the day on which the notice was transmitted or posted as the case m ay be.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices under this Agreement shall be in writing and may be sent by any means of communication to the party being served. The date of service shall be deemed to be the day the notice was sent.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for registered or recorded delivery and allowing 'any means of communication' creates ambiguity and potential disputes over whether notice was actually received. Also, removes the requirement that it be sent to the signatory of the agreement. This contradicts the principle of ensuring reliable and verifiable communication.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 8(1) of the Electronic Communications Act 2000
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 8(1) allows for electronic signatures but does not mandate them. The original clause ensures a verifiable method of delivery, which the modified clause removes. Allowing 'any means of communication' without specifying requirements for proof of delivery introduces uncertainty and contradicts legal requirements for certainty in contractual notices.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandated a verifiable delivery method (registered or recorded post) to ensure receipt by the intended party. The changed text broadens the acceptable communication methods to 'any means', which introduces ambiguity and lacks assurance of delivery or receipt. This creates a misalignment in terminology and a potential legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the removal of verifiable delivery, while the Law Explanation points out the introduced uncertainty regarding proof of delivery, contradicting legal requirements for certainty in contractual notices. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is about Electronic Communications Act 2000, and doesn't directly address registered mail, the issue is the removal of reliable method of communication, which the law snippet doesn't directly contradict. The change makes it more difficult to prove effective notice, potentially undermining the enforceability of the notice provision, and thus creating a legally relevant contradiction under the definition provided.
